[{"role": "system", "content": "# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- animal type: dog
- fur color: light (cream/white)
- fur texture: shaggy/curly
- face visibility: fur covers eyes
- nose color: black
</features>
<kb>
- IF [animal type = dog AND fur color = light AND fur texture = shaggy/curly] THEN [35]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = grey AND eye color = orange] THEN [9]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = grey AND eye color = light/greenish] THEN [27]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 35 fires because the Query Image shows a dog with light-colored, shaggy/curly fur, which matches the observable features of the example for class 35.
</rule_check>
<response>35</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

Query Image:

raw_response_text:
<features>
- animal type: cat
- fur color: grey
- eye color: yellow/orange
- body posture: lying down
- background: outdoor, paved surface, plants
</features>
<kb>
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = grey AND eye color = yellow/orange AND body posture = sitting upright] THEN [class 9]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = grey AND eye color = light/white AND body posture = sitting upright] THEN [class 27]
- IF [animal type = dog AND fur color = light brown/cream AND body posture = lying down] THEN [class 35]
</kb>
<rule_check>
The Query Image shows a cat with grey fur and yellow/orange eyes, lying down. None of the rules perfectly match all features. However, class 9 is characterized by a cat with grey fur and yellow/orange eyes. The posture in the example for class 9 is sitting upright, while the query image shows the cat lying down. Class 27 also features a grey cat, but with light/white eyes. Class 35 is a dog. Given the options, the most distinguishing features for class 9 are the grey fur and yellow/orange eyes, which are present in the query image, making it the closest match despite the difference in posture.
</rule_check>
<response>9</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- animal type: cat
- fur color: solid grey
- eye color: light (yellowish/greenish)
- body shape: somewhat slender
- ears: pointed
- face shape: somewhat triangular
</features>
<kb>
- IF [animal type = dog AND fur color = light brown/cream AND fur texture = curly/wavy] THEN [35]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = solid grey AND eye color = orange/amber AND body shape = stocky/round AND face shape = round] THEN [9]
- IF [animal type = cat AND fur color = solid grey AND eye color = light (yellowish/greenish) AND body shape = somewhat slender AND face shape = somewhat triangular] THEN [27]
</kb>
<rule_check>
- The rule for class 27 fires because the Query Image shows a cat with solid grey fur, light (yellowish/greenish) eyes, a somewhat slender body, and a somewhat triangular face, which matches the features defined for class 27.
</rule_check>
<response>27</response>

original_messages.content:
# TASK
1. Analyze the provided labeled examples to identify the observable visual features that distinguish each class.
2. Examine the Query Image and compare it strictly against the provided examples.
3. Determine which label from the provided options best matches the Query Image.

# INSTRUCTIONS
- Base your decision only on observable visual evidence in the examples and the Query Image.
- Use the examples to infer discriminative visual patterns for each class.
- Do not use external world knowledge, hidden assumptions, or speculative attributes.
- Use only labels from the provided final options list.
- Choose exactly one final label.
- Output only the requested XML tags and not any aditional ones.
- Do not output any text outside the XML tags.
- Follow the condition-specific XML blocks first, then output exactly one final <response> block.
- The final line of the entire answer must be <response>final_class</response>.
- Never omit the final <response> block, even when another explanation block is required.

First list the critical observable features present only in the Query Image.
Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

The content of <response> must be exactly one label copied verbatim from the provided options list.

Query Image:
Classify this image using only the labeled examples above.
Valid labels: [35, 9, 27]
Follow the exact XML structure required by the system instructions.
